required.title: [Ongoing] Access to a garden, yard, or natural area

competence_markers.description: The student habitually asks "and then what?" when considering actions or changes — tracing consequences beyond the first link
The student can identify and describe at least three reinforcing feedback loops and three balancing feedback loops from their own experience
The student can trace the Yellowstone wolves chain of consequence from wolves to rivers, explaining each link
The student can describe a garden (or similar system) as a system — identifying components, connections, and feedback loops
The student recognizes unintended consequences in stories and real life and can explain why they occurred
The student demonstrates beginning awareness that the same patterns (reinforcing loops, balancing loops, chain reactions) appear in different systems
The student can describe their own body as a system with interconnected parts that affect each other

narration_prompts.detail: Give me three examples of systems from your life. What makes them systems instead of just collections of parts?

narration_prompts.prompt: Explain a reinforcing feedback loop using an example from your own experience.
narration_prompts.detail: How does the result of the action feed back into the action itself?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me the story of the Yellowstone wolves.
narration_prompts.detail: How did wolves change the rivers? How many connections can you trace between wolves and the river?

narration_prompts.prompt: Why is "and then what?" such an important question?
narration_prompts.detail: Give me an example where someone did not ask it and got a surprise.

narration_prompts.prompt: How is a garden a system?
narration_prompts.detail: What are the connections between soil, water, plants, insects, and the gardener?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a time when trying to fix a problem made it worse.
narration_prompts.detail: What happened? Why did the fix backfire? What would a systems thinker have done differently?

cross_connections.description: Food webs, habitats, and ecosystem interdependence are the most natural introduction to systems thinking. The Yellowstone wolves case study bridges biology and systems thinking directly.
The water cycle, erosion, and weather are all systems with interconnected components. Systems thinking provides the framework for understanding them.

Habits are feedback loops. Good habits create reinforcing positive cycles. Bad habits create reinforcing negative cycles. The student who sees this connection understands their own behavior as a system.
A garden or farm is a system. Managing it well requires understanding the connections between soil, water, plants, animals, and human decisions.
Chains of consequence can be counted and tracked. Feedback loops can be diagrammed. Systems thinking develops the habit of looking at patterns in data.
River valley civilizations depended on environmental systems (rivers, soil, climate). Understanding why civilizations rose and fell is a systems question.

content.why_this_matters:
When you push a row of dominoes, the first one knocks over the second, which knocks over the third, and so on down the line. But the real world is not a row of dominoes. In the real world, things push back. Effects loop around and become causes. A change in one place shows up somewhere else in a way nobody expected.

If you remove the wolves from a forest, the deer population grows. More deer eat more plants. Fewer plants mean less food for insects and birds. Fewer roots holding the soil mean more erosion. More erosion changes the river. The river change affects the fish. By removing one animal, you changed the river. Nobody planned that. Nobody expected it. But it happened — because everything in the forest is connected.

This packet is about learning to see those connections. Not just in nature, but everywhere: in your family, in your habits, in your body, in the economy, in history. The world is not made of separate, independent pieces. It is made of systems — collections of things that interact with each other, affect each other, and produce results that none of the individual pieces would produce alone.

Learning to think in systems is one of the most powerful mental tools you will ever develop. A person who sees only parts will always be surprised by consequences. A person who sees systems will not be surprised as often — because they learned to ask the most important question in the world: **"And then what?"**
content.what_this_develops:
1. **The habit of tracing consequences** — asking "and then what?" before assuming a change will have only its intended effect
2. **Chain-of-consequence thinking** — following a cause through multiple effects, including effects that loop back
3. **Recognition of feedback** — noticing when an action creates more of itself (reinforcing) or corrects itself (balancing)
4. **Seeing wholes, not just parts** — the understanding that a system behaves differently from its individual pieces
5. **The garden as a system** — direct observation of interconnection in a living system
6. **Early pattern recognition** — noticing that the same kinds of connections appear in different places
content.core_content:
### Part 1: What Is a System?

A system is a group of things that work together. Your body is a system — your heart, lungs, muscles, bones, and brain all work together to keep you alive. A garden is a system — soil, water, sun, plants, insects, and worms all work together to produce food. A family is a system — each person affects the others.

What makes a system more than just a collection of parts is the connections between the parts. A pile of car parts is not a car. But when those parts are connected in the right way, the system — the car — can do something that none of the parts can do alone. It can drive.

This is an important idea: **a system can do things that none of its parts can do alone.** Your brain cells cannot think by themselves. But billions of brain cells connected together can think, dream, remember, and imagine. No single ant knows how to build a colony. But thousands of ants working together build complex structures with ventilation, food storage, and nurseries. The behavior of the system is more than the sum of its parts.

### Part 2: Chains of Consequence

When you do something, it causes something else to happen. And that something else causes another thing. And so on.

If you leave your bicycle in the rain, it rusts. If it rusts, the chain stiffens. If the chain stiffens, it is hard to pedal. If it is hard to pedal, you do not ride it. If you do not ride it, you do not get exercise. One forgotten bicycle in the rain, and eventually your health is affected. That is a chain of consequence.

Most of the time, we only think about the first link in the chain. "I left my bike out — it got wet." We do not think about the second, third, or fourth links. But they happen whether we think about them or not.

**The most important question in systems thinking is: "And then what?"**

You decide to cut down a tree in the yard. And then what? The birds that nested in it leave. And then what? The insects the birds ate multiply. And then what? The insects eat your garden plants. And then what? You need to buy pesticide. And then what? The pesticide kills the bees that pollinate your other plants. And then what?

This does not mean you should never cut down a tree. It means you should think about what happens next — and next after that — before you act.

### Part 3: Feedback — When Effects Become Causes

The most powerful thing in systems thinking is the feedback loop. A feedback loop happens when the result of an action loops back and affects the action itself.

**Reinforcing feedback (snowball effect):** You practice piano. You get better. Getting better makes it more fun. More fun makes you practice more. More practice makes you get even better. The improvement feeds on itself — each result pushes the process further in the same direction.

But reinforcing feedback works in both directions. You skip a day of practice. Being rusty makes it less fun. Less fun makes you skip more days. More skipping makes you more rusty. The decline also feeds on itself.

**Balancing feedback (thermostat effect):** You eat a big meal. Your stomach feels full. Feeling full makes you stop eating. This is balancing feedback — the system corrects itself. When you get too cold, you shiver — and shivering produces heat, warming you up. When a population of rabbits grows too large, there is not enough food, and the population shrinks. The system pushes back toward a balance point.

You experience both kinds of feedback every day. When you notice them, you start to understand why some things spiral out of control and why other things stay stable.

### Part 4: The Wolves and the Rivers

In 1995, wolves were reintroduced to Yellowstone National Park after being absent for 70 years. What happened next is one of the most famous examples of how systems work.

**Without wolves:** Elk populations were unchecked. They grazed heavily along riverbanks, eating young willows and aspens. Without trees, riverbanks eroded. Rivers widened and became shallower. Beavers, which need willows to build dams, disappeared.

**With wolves:** Elk behavior changed — they avoided areas where wolves could ambush them, particularly valleys and riverbanks. Young trees grew back. Roots stabilized riverbanks. Rivers narrowed and deepened. Beavers returned and built dams, creating ponds that supported fish, amphibians, and birds. Berries grew on the recovering vegetation, feeding bears. Carcasses from wolf kills fed ravens, eagles, and insects.

Wolves changed the rivers. Not by digging or damming — by eating elk, which changed elk behavior, which let trees grow, which stabilized banks. The connection between wolves and rivers runs through five or six intermediate links. Nobody would have predicted it without understanding the system.

This is what systems thinking teaches you to see: the connections that are real but not obvious.

### Part 5: The Garden as a System

If you have a garden, you have a systems thinking laboratory.

**The soil** feeds the plants. But the plants also feed the soil — their roots create channels for water and air, their fallen leaves decompose into organic matter, and they support the fungi and bacteria that cycle nutrients. The soil and plants are not separate things — they are a system that sustains each other.

**The insects** are part of the system. Bees pollinate flowers, making fruit possible. Ladybugs eat aphids that would damage plants. Earthworms process organic matter and aerate the soil. Even insects you might consider pests play roles — they feed birds, which control other pest populations.

**Water** cycles through the garden: rain falls, soaks into soil, is taken up by roots, transpires through leaves into the air, forms clouds, and falls again. The garden is a small piece of the water cycle.

**Your decisions** are part of the system too. If you add compost, you feed the soil organisms, which feed the plants. If you use pesticide, you kill pests — but also kill the beneficial insects that were controlling other pests. If you pull all the weeds, you remove plants that were holding soil in place and providing habitat for beneficial insects.

Every action in the garden has consequences beyond the obvious. Learning to see those consequences is learning to think in systems.

### Part 6: Your Body as a System

Your body is one of the most complex systems you will ever encounter. Every part depends on every other part.

**Sleep** affects everything. When you do not sleep enough, your body does not repair itself, your brain does not process what you learned, your mood suffers, and your immune system weakens. One missing piece — sleep — degrades the entire system.

**Food** is not just fuel. The nutrients in your food become the building materials for your cells, the chemicals that run your brain, and the energy that powers your muscles. What you eat affects how you think, feel, move, and recover. It is not a separate decision from your health — it is your health.

**Exercise** does not just make your muscles stronger. It improves your heart's function, increases blood flow to your brain (improving learning), strengthens your bones, improves your sleep, and regulates your mood. One activity — moving your body — improves five or six other systems at the same time.

**Stress** affects the whole system too. When you are stressed, your body diverts energy from growth, repair, and digestion toward immediate survival responses. Short-term stress is normal and useful. Long-term stress degrades every system in the body.

Your body is not a collection of parts that can be treated separately. It is a system — and understanding it as a system is the foundation of health (connected to 1.6.8 — Medical Sovereignty).

### Part 7: Unintended Consequences — When Helping Hurts

Sometimes people try to fix a problem and accidentally create a new one. This happens when they do not think about the system — only about the part they can see.

**The cobra problem:** In colonial India, the British government was worried about venomous cobras in Delhi. They offered a bounty — a reward for every dead cobra brought in. At first, it worked. People killed cobras and collected bounties. But then people started breeding cobras — to kill them and collect the bounty. When the government discovered this and canceled the bounty, the cobra breeders released their now-worthless snakes. The cobra population ended up larger than before.

**The cane toad problem:** In Australia, cane beetles were destroying sugar cane crops. In 1935, cane toads were introduced from Hawaii to eat the beetles. The toads did not eat the beetles. Instead, they multiplied rapidly, poisoned native animals that tried to eat them, and spread across the continent. They are still a major ecological problem almost a century later.

**The lesson:** When you intervene in a system, the system responds — and its response may not be what you expected. The person who asks "and then what?" before acting is less likely to create unintended consequences than the person who sees only the immediate problem and the obvious solution.

### Part 8: Patterns That Repeat

One of the most useful things about systems thinking is that the same patterns show up in completely different places:

**The snowball pattern (reinforcing feedback)** shows up in:
- Practice making you better, which makes you practice more
- A small crack in a road getting bigger as water freezes in it
- A rumor spreading — each person who hears it tells two more people
- Compound interest — money earning money earning more money

**The thermostat pattern (balancing feedback)** shows up in:
- Your body temperature staying stable even when the air temperature changes
- A predator population declining when prey becomes scarce
- A popular restaurant raising prices when too many customers create long waits
- Feeling thirsty when your body needs water

**The chain reaction pattern** shows up in:
- Wolves changing rivers through elk and trees
- A traffic jam spreading backward from a single slowdown
- One sick person infecting others, who infect others, who infect others

When you learn to see these patterns, you start seeing them everywhere. That is the beginning of systems thinking.
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: The "And Then What?" Game [Ongoing — Full Packet]
Whenever a decision is being made — in a story, in the garden, in the family — practice the "and then what?" chain. Start with the first action and follow consequences as far as you can. Make it a game: take turns adding the next link in the chain. The more you practice, the longer and more creative the chains become.

### Study 2: The Garden System Map [Early Packet]
Draw a map of your garden (or yard, or a local park) as a system. Put the major components in circles: soil, water, plants, insects, birds, sun, the gardener (you). Draw arrows showing connections: sun feeds plants, plants feed soil, soil feeds plants, insects pollinate plants, birds eat insects. Discuss: what happens if you remove one of these circles?

### Study 3: Feedback in Your Life [Mid-Packet]
Together, identify three reinforcing feedback loops and three balancing feedback loops in the student's daily life. Examples: "The more I read, the better I read, the more I enjoy reading" (reinforcing). "When I eat too much sugar, I feel sick, so I stop eating sugar" (balancing). Draw them as loops with arrows.

### Study 4: The Yellowstone Wolves [Mid-Packet]
Watch the Yellowstone wolves documentary (or read about it). Then draw the chain of connections: wolves -> elk behavior -> tree growth -> riverbank stability -> river shape -> beavers -> ponds -> fish and birds. Discuss: could anyone have predicted all of this? What does this teach about how systems work?

### Study 5: The Unintended Consequences Collection [Ongoing]
Start a collection of unintended consequences — from stories, from history, from daily life. When you encounter one, add it to a notebook. For each, identify: what was the intended action? What was the unintended result? Why did it happen? Over time, the student begins to see patterns in how good intentions produce bad outcomes when systems are not understood.

### Study 6: Body System Connections [Mid-to-Late Packet]
Map the connections between sleep, food, exercise, and mood. The student tracks (informally) how they feel on days with good sleep versus poor sleep, after exercise versus without, after different foods. The goal is not a scientific study but a personal awareness that body systems are connected. Draw it as a system map showing the connections.

### Study 7: The Lorax Discussion [Late Packet]
Read *The Lorax* together. Discuss: what system was the Once-ler part of? What connections did he ignore? What feedback was he receiving (the Lorax's warnings) and why did he ignore it? What would a systems thinker have done differently? This is a story about what happens when someone sees only the part of the system that benefits them.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Consequence Tracing
**Developing well:** The student spontaneously asks "and then what?" or traces consequences beyond the first link. Can follow a chain of three or more consequences in a familiar system. Notices when their own actions produce unexpected results.
**Developing slowly:** Stops at the first link. Cannot extend a consequence chain even with prompting. Does not connect actions to distant effects.

### 2. Feedback Recognition
**Developing well:** Can identify examples of reinforcing and balancing feedback in their own life without prompting. Uses the patterns to understand why some things spiral and others stabilize.
**Developing slowly:** Cannot distinguish feedback types. Does not see loops — only linear chains.

### 3. System Awareness
**Developing well:** Describes a garden, ecosystem, or their body as a system with connected parts. Understands that removing or changing one part affects others.
**Developing slowly:** Sees things in isolation. Does not spontaneously notice connections between parts.

### 4. Unintended Consequence Awareness
**Developing well:** Recognizes when actions produce results that were not intended. Can explain why the unintended result occurred in terms of system connections.
**Developing slowly:** Finds unintended consequences surprising but cannot explain why they happened.

### 5. Pattern Recognition
**Developing well:** Notices that the same patterns (snowball effects, self-correcting loops) appear in different systems. May say: "That's like what happens with..." drawing analogies across contexts.
**Developing slowly:** Each example feels unique. No transfer between contexts.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Making It Too Abstract
Systems thinking vocabulary (feedback, reinforcing, balancing) can make this packet feel technical and abstract. Resist this. At Stretching Branches, the concepts are introduced through stories, games, and observations — not definitions. If the student can recognize a snowball effect in practice but cannot define "reinforcing feedback loop," they are doing fine. If they can define it but cannot recognize one in real life, they are not.

### Ignoring the Garden
If you have access to a garden or natural area and are not using it as a primary teaching tool for this packet, you are missing the best resource available. A garden makes every concept tangible.

### Stopping at Wolves
The Yellowstone wolves case study is compelling and will capture the student's imagination. But if it becomes the only example of systems thinking, the student has learned about wolves, not about systems. Ensure they see the same patterns in their own life, their body, their habits, and their family.

### Forcing Long Chains Too Early
For younger Stretching Branches students (4-6), a consequence chain of two or three links is excellent. Do not push for longer chains before the student is ready. The length will naturally increase with age and practice. The habit is what matters, not the chain length.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: What Is a System
**Strong:** Gives multiple examples and explains why they are systems (connected parts that work together to produce something). May articulate that a system does things its parts cannot do alone.
**Weak:** Gives an example but cannot explain what makes it a system.

### Prompt 2: Reinforcing Feedback
**Strong:** Describes a specific loop from their own life with clear cause-and-effect links that feed back into each other. May describe both positive and negative spirals.
**Weak:** Describes a simple cause-and-effect without the loop — no feedback, just a chain.

### Prompt 3: Yellowstone Wolves
**Strong:** Traces the chain with multiple links, explaining each connection. Shows wonder at the unexpected connection between wolves and rivers.
**Weak:** "Wolves came back and things got better." No chain, no specific connections.

### Prompt 4: "And Then What?"
**Strong:** Gives a specific example with at least three consequences in the chain. Explains why the person was surprised — they did not think about the system.
**Weak:** Cannot produce a chain longer than one link, or gives an example that is linear rather than systemic.

### Prompt 5: Garden as System
**Strong:** Identifies multiple components and traces connections between them, including at least one feedback loop. May describe what would happen if a component were removed.
**Weak:** Lists components without connections. "The garden has soil and plants and water."

### Prompt 6: Unintended Consequences
**Strong:** Tells a clear story with intended action, unintended result, and explanation of why the unintended result occurred (system connections the person did not see).
**Weak:** Can tell the story but cannot explain why the result was unintended.
